title: Czech svíčková-style beef goulash
subtitle: Czech braised beef in a dark onion and caraway sauce
excerpt: Beef chuck braised low and slow with a mountain of softened onions, caraway, and sweet paprika until the sauce is thick and almost black with caramelised onion. The Czech pub staple, served with bread dumplings and a spoonful of crème fraîche.
sourceNotes: Based on the Czech guláš tradition as documented in Jiri Kopac and Pavel Maurer, 'Czech Cuisine' (1999) and cross-referenced with pub-kitchen practice. Czech guláš differs from Hungarian in its heavier reliance on onions and caraway, the absence of sour cream in the sauce itself, and its association with bread dumplings (knedlíky) rather than egg noodles.

content.text: Czech guláš is built on a ratio that surprises most cooks: roughly equal weights of onion and meat. The onions go in first and cook slowly until deeply golden, almost jammy, before the beef is added. This long onion base is what gives Czech goulash its dark, thick sauce, there are no thickeners beyond the onion itself, which breaks down entirely during the braise. Caraway seeds are the characteristic flavour, along with sweet paprika and a small amount of marjoram.

The cut of beef matters. Chuck or shin gives the best result: connective tissue that softens during the long braise and thickens the sauce further. Lean cuts remain dry. The finished goulash should be scoopable rather than soupy.
In Czech pubs it is served with knedlíky (bread or potato dumplings) and a spoonful of crème fraîche on top. Crusty bread works equally well if dumplings are not available.

Melt {{lard}} of lard or oil in a wide Dutch oven over a medium-low heat. Add {{onion}} of sliced onions with a pinch of salt and cook slowly for 30 to 35 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply golden and soft. Do not rush this step. It is where the sauce depth comes from.

Push the onions to the side and increase the heat to medium-high. Add {{beef-chuck}} of beef chuck in batches and brown on all sides. Return all the beef to the pot. Add {{garlic}} crushed garlic, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{caraway-seeds}} of caraway seeds, and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée. Stir to coat. Pour in {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, add {{bay-leaves}} bay leaves and {{dried-marjoram}} of dried marjoram. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper-ground}} of pepper.

Bring to a simmer, cover tightly, and cook on the lowest heat for 1.5 to 2 hours until the beef is completely tender and the sauce has thickened to a dark, coating consistency. Check every 30 minutes and add a splash of water if the sauce is reducing too fast. Remove the bay leaves. Rest for 10 minutes before serving with crème fraîche and bread dumplings or crusty bread.

items.fix: Uncover and simmer briskly for 10 to 15 minutes to reduce. The sauce thickens from onion breakdown, not starch, volume reduction is the only fix.
items.cause: Not enough onion, or lid too tight preventing evaporation

items.fix: Continue braising with the lid on. Chuck at 3 cm cubes takes 90 minutes minimum at a proper simmer.
items.cause: Temperature too low, or chuck cut too large

items.fix: Always add paprika after reducing the heat slightly and stir quickly. It should sizzle gently, not smoke.
items.cause: Paprika scorched when added to the hot pan

content.text: Guláš is the most ordered dish in Czech pubs, the pivnice and hospoda. It has been since the Austro-Hungarian period, when the dish arrived from Hungary and was adapted to Czech taste. The Czech version dropped the sour cream that Hungarians stir through the sauce. In its place came a heavier onion base, more caraway, and the standard side of knedlíky. It is canteen food in the best sense: cheap, filling, better as leftovers, and well liked by all.
